sections.title: 1. Regular Verb Conjugation
sections.description: In German, verbs change their endings based on the subject. We take the verb stem (remove -en from the infinitive) and add these specific endings:

dialogues.speaker: Alice
dialogues.german: Ich komme aus Brasilien. Das ist mein Freund, er kommt aus Russland.
dialogues.english: I come from Brazil. This is my friend, he comes from Russia.
dialogues.tip: Teacher's Tip: Notice how "er" (he) always takes the -t ending.

dialogues.speaker: Marc
dialogues.english: And where do you all live?

dialogues.speaker: Alice & Yuri
dialogues.german: Wir wohnen ganz in der Nähe. Wo arbeitest du?
dialogues.english: We live very close by. Where do you work?
dialogues.tip: Grammar Alert: For verbs ending in -t or -d like arbeiten, we add an extra -e- (arbeit-e-st) to make it pronounceable.

sections.title: 2. Essential Irregular Verbs: "Sein" & "Haben"
sections.description: These verbs are the foundation of the language. They do not follow the standard rules and must be memorized.

sections.title: 3. Preferences and Wishes: "Möchten" & "Mögen"
sections.description: Möchten (Would like) - Used for ordering or requests. Mögen (To like) - General likes/dislikes

examples.english: My parents do not like fish.
examples.note: Note: "Mögen" changes its stem vowel from ö to a in singular forms (mag).

sections.title: 4. Knowing Facts: "Wissen"
sections.description: This is used for factual knowledge (knowing "that" or knowing a location).

examples.german: Wissen Sie, wo der Bahnhof ist?
examples.english: Do you know where the train station is?
